skill.name: terminal-title
skill.description: Updates terminal window titles with an emoji, project name, and active topic using local Bash or PowerShell scripts.
skill.summary: Keeps terminal titles aligned with the current project and conversation topic.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many external command, filesystem, obfuscation, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns. Review confirms the scripts are local title-setting helpers with input sanitization and no evidence of network access, credential collection, or malicious intent. The remaining concern is automatic, pre-approved script execution that writes terminal escape sequences, so publication should include a clear warning.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Local Script Execution Requires User Trust
medium_findings.description: The skill asks users to pre-approve Bash or PowerShell execution for bundled title scripts. The scripts appear scoped and sanitized, but automatic invocation on session start and topic changes increases trust requirements.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly requests pre-approved command execution and silent invocation. The risk is confirmed, although the observed scripts have a narrow purpose.

medium_findings.title: Terminal Escape Sequence Output
medium_findings.description: The scripts emit terminal title escape sequences to stdout, Write-Host, or /dev/tty. Inputs are stripped of control characters and length-limited, which lowers the chance of escape injection.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly emits ANSI title sequences, but it removes control characters from user-facing inputs before output.

low_findings.title: Static Obfuscation and Weak Crypto Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The hex-like patterns are PowerShell regular expression ranges for control character removal. The weak cryptography and reconnaissance matches occur in normal title-management documentation, with no evidence of cryptographic use or network scanning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain sanitization syntax or ordinary documentation. No evidence found for encoded payloads, cryptographic operations, or network reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Home Settings Path Mention Is User Configuration Guidance
low_findings.description: The hidden home path reference tells users where to add Claude permission settings. It is not code that reads, writes, or exfiltrates that file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The location is documentation for setup only. No evidence found that the skill reads secrets or modifies the settings file itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pre-Approved Shell and PowerShell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs users to allow commands that execute local Bash and PowerShell scripts. This is useful for automation but should be disclosed before publishing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns are documented directly. They execute local files and do not show remote download or dynamic command construction.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct Terminal Device Output
dangerous_patterns.description: The Unix script writes an escape sequence to /dev/tty and falls back to stdout. This is a legitimate title update method but can affect terminal display state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly targets terminal output. Sanitization reduces impact, but the behavior is still a sensitive terminal-control pattern.

content.value_statement: Long AI sessions can make terminal windows hard to identify. This skill updates the title with the active project, topic, and emoji context.
actual_capabilities: Sets terminal window titles with project and topic context.
Reads an optional emoji from the terminal environment.
Supports Unix-like shells through a Bash helper script.
Supports Windows terminals through a PowerShell helper script.
Removes control characters and limits title component length.
Provides workflow guidance for session starts and topic changes.
limitations: Requires permission to run the bundled local script.
Depends on terminal support for title escape sequences.
Project and topic names rely on AI context detection.
Does not manage terminal tabs outside the active session.

use_cases.title: Track Active Coding Sessions
use_cases.description: Show the current project and work topic in each terminal window.
use_cases.target_user: Developers using multiple AI coding sessions

use_cases.title: Separate Work and Personal Contexts
use_cases.description: Use an environment emoji to distinguish work directories from personal projects.
use_cases.target_user: Engineers switching between client, job, and hobby repositories

use_cases.title: Keep Pairing Sessions Oriented
use_cases.description: Update titles when the discussion moves between debugging, documentation, tests, or features.

prompt_templates.title: Set a Basic Title
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a known project and topic.
prompt_templates.prompt: Set my terminal title for this project as My App and the topic as Authentication.

prompt_templates.title: Use Current Context
prompt_templates.scenario: Let the assistant infer project context from the repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the terminal title using the current project name and this conversation topic.

prompt_templates.title: Change Topics During Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Refresh the title when the task changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: We are moving from frontend fixes to database migrations. Update the terminal title for the new topic.

prompt_templates.title: Review Title Automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced check before enabling automatic invocation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review how this skill sets terminal titles and explain what permission it needs before I enable it.

output_examples.input: Set the title for Skills Repository while working on Terminal Title.
output_examples.output: The terminal window title changes to show the work emoji, Skills Repository, and Terminal Title.

output_examples.input: The discussion changes from CSS refactor to API tests.
output_examples.output: The terminal title updates to the same project name with API Tests as the topic.

output_examples.input: Start a session in a personal dotfiles repository without an emoji setting.
output_examples.output: The title uses the fallback emoji, the dotfiles project name, and Claude Code as the topic.

best_practices: Review the local scripts before granting command permissions.
Keep project and topic names short enough for your terminal title bar.
Use a trusted environment variable if you customize the emoji.
anti_patterns: Do not grant broad command permissions beyond the bundled title scripts.
Do not use untrusted text as a title without sanitization.
Do not expect title updates in terminals that ignore title escape sequences.

faq.question: What does this skill change?
faq.answer: It changes the visible terminal window title. It does not change project files.

faq.question: Does it need command execution permission?
faq.answer: Yes. It runs a local Bash or PowerShell script to send the title sequence.

faq.question: Does it send data over the network?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed files do not contain network requests.

faq.question: How does it choose the project name?
faq.answer: The assistant infers a human-friendly name from context, repository details, and directory names.

faq.question: Can it work on Windows?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes a PowerShell helper for Windows terminals.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: It asks for pre-approved local script execution. The scripts look narrow, but users should review them first.
